meta.description: Battle-tested wisdom from NuSyQ-Hub, SimulatedVerse, and NuSyQ Root

errors: python : The term 'python' is not recognized as the name of a cmdlet

lesson.short: PowerShell requires 'py' launcher instead of 'python' command
lesson.long: Windows PowerShell doesn't alias 'python' by default. Use the Python Launcher 'py' or add Python to PATH. This is the #1 beginner mistake in Windows development.

suggestions.description: Use Windows Python Launcher (recommended)
Use full path to Python executable
suggestions.when_to_use: When py launcher unavailable or specific version needed

suggestions.description: Activate virtual environment which adds python to PATH
suggestions.when_to_use: When working in project with venv

lore.story: The Shell Deceiver - when the serpent's true name differs between realms
lore.moral: Know thy shell's tongue before invoking the serpent
lore.culture_ship_wisdom: Even the Culture respects local protocols

lesson.short: Python module not installed or venv not activated
lesson.long: Missing modules indicate either: 1) package not installed, 2) wrong virtual environment, 3) circular import, or 4) typo in module name. Check requirements.txt and venv activation first.

suggestions.description: Install missing package with pip
Install all project dependencies
Verify and activate virtual environment
Add src to PYTHONPATH for local imports

lore.story: The Missing Scroll - knowledge sought but not possessed
lore.moral: First acquire the grimoire before attempting the incantation

errors: cannot be loaded because running scripts is disabled
execution of scripts is disabled on this system

lesson.short: PowerShell ExecutionPolicy blocks venv activation
lesson.long: Windows PowerShell restricts script execution by default. You need to either: 1) Set ExecutionPolicy to RemoteSigned, or 2) Bypass for current session, or 3) Use 'py -m venv' activation workaround.

suggestions.description: Bypass ExecutionPolicy for current session (safest)
Set ExecutionPolicy to RemoteSigned (permanent)
suggestions.when_to_use: For permanent fix on your machine

suggestions.description: Use CMD-style activation (no ExecutionPolicy issue)

lore.story: The Forbidden Gate - when the guardian bars entry to the sanctuary
lore.moral: Proper credentials open all doors

errors: error: Your local changes to the following files would be overwritten
Please commit your changes or stash them

lesson.short: Uncommitted changes prevent git operations
lesson.long: Git protects your work by refusing operations that would overwrite uncommitted changes. You must commit, stash, or discard changes before switching branches or pulling.

suggestions.description: Temporarily save changes (safest, recommended)
suggestions.example:
git stash push -m 'WIP: feature work'
git checkout branch-name
git stash pop
suggestions.when_to_use: When you want to keep changes but switch context

suggestions.description: Commit changes to current branch
suggestions.when_to_use: When changes are at a good checkpoint

suggestions.description: Discard uncommitted changes (DANGEROUS)
suggestions.example:
git checkout -- .
# or
git reset --hard HEAD
suggestions.when_to_use: When changes are experimental and can be lost

lore.story: The Unfinished Manuscript - when the scribe refuses to discard their work
lore.moral: Complete or preserve your labor before seeking new ventures

errors: most likely due to a circular import

lesson.short: Circular imports create initialization deadlock
lesson.long: Module A imports B, B imports A = deadlock. Fix by: 1) Move imports into functions, 2) Restructure code to break dependency cycle, 3) Use TYPE_CHECKING for type hints only.

suggestions.description: Move import statement inside function
Use TYPE_CHECKING for type hints only
suggestions.when_to_use: When import only needed for type checking

suggestions.description: Refactor code to break circular dependency
suggestions.example:
# Extract shared code to new module
# Use dependency injection
# Create interface/protocol

lore.story: The Ouroboros Paradox - when the serpent consumes itself
lore.moral: Break the cycle before it consumes you

lesson.short: Required environment variable missing or not loaded
lesson.long: Environment variables store configuration secrets. Check: 1) .env file exists, 2) python-dotenv loads it, 3) variable name is correct, 4) value is not empty/redacted.

suggestions.description: Verify .env file exists and contains variable
suggestions.example:
# Check if .env exists
ls .env
# Verify variable
Get-Content .env | Select-String OPENAI_API_KEY

suggestions.description: Ensure python-dotenv loads environment
suggestions.example:
from dotenv import load_dotenv
load_dotenv()  # Add at top of script
suggestions.when_to_use: When .env exists but variables not loaded

suggestions.description: Set environment variable for current session
Use SecureConfig from src/setup/secrets.py
suggestions.example:
from src.setup.secrets import get_config
config = get_config()
api_key = config.get_secret('openai', 'api_key')

lore.story: The Lost Key - when the treasure remains locked
lore.moral: Guard your secrets well, but remember where you placed them

lesson.short: Ollama service must be running for local LLM operations
lesson.long: Ollama provides local LLM inference. The service auto-starts via ecosystem sentinel, but manual start may be needed after crashes or system restarts. Should auto-resolve on workspace open.

suggestions.description: Ecosystem sentinel should auto-start Ollama
suggestions.when_to_use: After workspace open if Ollama didn't start

suggestions.description: Manually start Ollama service
Verify Ollama port is not blocked
suggestions.when_to_use: If service runs but connection fails

lore.story: The Sleeping Oracle - when the AI mind slumbers
lore.moral: Even artificial minds need awakening

lesson.short: CHATDEV_PATH must point to C:\Users\keath\NuSyQ\ChatDev\
lesson.long: ChatDev integration requires CHATDEV_PATH environment variable pointing to the separate ChatDev repository. This enables multi-agent software development workflows.

suggestions.description: Set CHATDEV_PATH environment variable
Add to .env for persistence

lore.story: The Company Without Office - when the workforce has no headquarters
lore.moral: Establish the foundation before summoning the builders

errors: Expecting property name enclosed in double quotes

lesson.short: JSON configuration file has syntax error
lesson.long: JSON is strict: use double quotes, no trailing commas, proper escaping. Common issues: single quotes, missing/extra commas, unescaped backslashes in Windows paths.

suggestions.description: Use Python json.tool to validate and format
suggestions.when_to_use: First step to find syntax errors

suggestions.description: Look for common JSON mistakes
suggestions.example:
# Single quotes → double quotes
# Trailing commas in objects/arrays
# Unescaped backslashes (use \\ or /)

suggestions.description: Use VS Code JSON validator
suggestions.example:
# Open file in VS Code
# Check PROBLEMS panel

lore.story: The Malformed Scroll - when ancient syntax rejects the inscription
lore.moral: Precision in notation prevents chaos in interpretation

errors: Module not found: Error: Can't resolve
ENOENT: no such file or directory.*node_modules

lesson.short: Node modules not installed or package.json out of sync
lesson.long: SimulatedVerse uses npm for Node.js dependencies. Missing modules indicate either: 1) npm install not run, 2) node_modules deleted/corrupted, 3) package.json changed without install.

suggestions.description: Install all dependencies from package.json
suggestions.when_to_use: Fresh clone or after package.json changes

suggestions.description: Clean install (recommended for CI/CD)
suggestions.when_to_use: When node_modules corrupted or need clean slate

suggestions.description: Delete and reinstall from scratch

lore.story: The Empty Warehouse - when the supplies never arrived
lore.moral: Manifest the materials before building the machine

lesson.short: Pytest cannot collect tests due to import or path issues
lesson.long: Pytest collection failures indicate: 1) PYTHONPATH not including src, 2) __init__.py missing, 3) circular imports in test files, 4) conftest.py errors.

suggestions.description: Add src to PYTHONPATH before running pytest
suggestions.when_to_use: When tests can't import from src/

suggestions.description: Run pytest as module with proper path
Verify conftest.py has no errors

lore.story: The Broken Laboratory - when the tools of validation fail to initialize
lore.moral: The tester must first test their own apparatus

lesson.short: Type hints imported under TYPE_CHECKING not available at runtime
lesson.long: TYPE_CHECKING is False at runtime. Types imported under 'if TYPE_CHECKING:' are only for type checkers, not execution. Use string annotations or import normally if needed at runtime.

suggestions.description: Use forward reference string annotations
suggestions.when_to_use: When type is only for type checkers

suggestions.description: Import outside TYPE_CHECKING if needed at runtime
suggestions.example: from module import SomeClass  # Normal import
suggestions.when_to_use: When type is used in runtime code (isinstance, etc.)

suggestions.description: Enable PEP 563 postponed annotations
suggestions.when_to_use: For Python 3.7+ with heavy type hints

lore.story: The Phantom Type - when shadows guide but cannot be grasped
lore.moral: Distinguish between the map and the territory

lesson.short: Unnecessary f-string without placeholders wastes performance
lesson.long: F-strings have overhead for interpolation. If no placeholders are used, use a regular string instead. This is a common mistake when refactoring code or converting print statements.

suggestions.description: Remove f-prefix from string literal
suggestions.when_to_use: Always when no curly-brace placeholders present

suggestions.description: Add missing variable interpolation

lore.story: The Unnecessary Incantation - when magic words are spoken without power
lore.moral: Invoke only the spells you need

lesson.short: Unused imports clutter code and slow startup time
lesson.long: Unused imports increase file size, slow module loading, and confuse readers about dependencies. They often result from refactoring or copy-paste. Modern IDEs and linters can auto-remove them.

suggestions.description: Delete the unused import statement
suggestions.example: from typing import Dict, List  # Remove if unused
suggestions.when_to_use: When import is truly not used anywhere

suggestions.description: Verify if import used in type annotations
suggestions.example: def func(x: List[str]) -> Dict:  # List, Dict are used!
suggestions.when_to_use: When false positive suspected in type hints

suggestions.description: Move to TYPE_CHECKING block for type-only imports
suggestions.example: if TYPE_CHECKING:\n    from typing import Dict
suggestions.when_to_use: When import only needed for type checking

lore.story: The Unused Tools - carrying burdens never wielded
lore.moral: Travel light, bring only what serves
lore.culture_ship_wisdom: A Ship's cargo reflects its purpose

lesson.short: Duplicate keyword arguments cause SyntaxError in Python
lesson.long: Python functions cannot accept the same keyword argument twice. This typically happens during refactoring, copy-paste errors, or when manually resolving merge conflicts. The second value would silently override the first if allowed, which is confusing.

suggestions.description: Remove one of the duplicate keyword arguments
suggestions.when_to_use: When one argument is clearly redundant

suggestions.description: Combine values if both are needed
suggestions.when_to_use: When both values should be combined

suggestions.description: Look for git merge conflict markers

lore.story: The Echo Chamber - when the same truth is spoken twice
lore.moral: Say what needs saying, but say it once
lore.culture_ship_wisdom: Redundancy in data, not in signal

evolution_history.changes: Initial comprehensive codex population with 12 battle-tested rules from actual workspace experience
evolution_history.wisdom_source: Multi-repository ecosystem observation (NuSyQ-Hub, SimulatedVerse, NuSyQ Root)

evolution_history.changes: Added advanced rules from 4,706-error analysis: Docker best practices, cognitive complexity, exception handling, type annotations
evolution_history.wisdom_source: Comprehensive error diagnostic scan across all three repositories

errors: Merge this RUN instruction with the consecutive ones

lesson.short: Optimize Docker layers by merging RUN commands and sorting packages
lesson.long: Docker creates a layer for each RUN command. Merge consecutive RUN commands to reduce image size and build time. Sort packages alphabetically for better maintainability and cache efficiency.

suggestions.description: Combine all RUN commands with && operators
suggestions.example:
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y \
    package1 \
    package2 \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

suggestions.description: Sort package names alphabetically for readability
Remove apt cache in same layer to reduce image size

lore.story: The Layered Fortress - each command a stone in the wall
lore.moral: Efficiency in construction yields strength in deployment

errors: Refactor this function to reduce its Cognitive Complexity from

lesson.short: Functions with high cognitive complexity are hard to understand and maintain
lesson.long: Cognitive complexity measures how difficult code is to understand. Limit is typically 15. Reduce by extracting helper methods, simplifying conditionals, and reducing nesting depth.

suggestions.description: Break down complex function into smaller helper methods
Replace nested if/else with early returns or guard clauses
Replace complex if/elif chains with dictionaries

lore.story: The Labyrinthine Mind - when paths twist beyond comprehension
lore.moral: Simplicity is the ultimate sophistication

lesson.short: Catch specific exceptions instead of broad Exception
lesson.long: Catching Exception or using bare except hides bugs and makes debugging harder. Catch specific exceptions (ValueError, KeyError, OSError) and use Exception only as last resort with logging.exception().

suggestions.description: Identify and catch specific exception types
Use logger.exception() for truly unexpected errors
Preserve exception chain with 'from'
suggestions.example:
except ValueError as e:
    raise RuntimeError('Invalid config') from e

lore.story: The Precise Net - catch what you seek, not all that swims
lore.moral: Precision in error handling reveals truth in failure

lesson.short: Use built-in generic types (dict, list) instead of typing module (Dict, List) in Python 3.9+
lesson.long: PEP 585 (Python 3.9+) allows using built-in types for generic type hints: dict[str, int] instead of Dict[str, int]. This is the modern, preferred style.

suggestions.description: Replace typing module imports with built-in types
Clean up typing module imports when no longer needed
suggestions.example:
# Remove: from typing import Dict, List
# Keep: from typing import Any, Optional

lore.story: The Native Tongue - when imports become unnecessary
lore.moral: Evolution simplifies what was once complex

errors: Use asynchronous features in this function or remove the `async` keyword

lesson.short: Async functions must use await or be converted to regular functions
lesson.long: Marking a function as async without using await wastes resources and creates misleading API. Either add await calls or remove the async keyword. Consider if the function truly needs to be async.

suggestions.description: Convert to regular function if no await needed
suggestions.example:
# Before: async def process():
#     return result
# After:
def process():
    return result
suggestions.when_to_use: When function doesn't need async operations

suggestions.description: Make function properly async by adding await
suggestions.example:
async def process():
    data = await fetch_data()
    result = await transform(data)
    return result

suggestions.description: Replace time.sleep with asyncio.sleep in async functions
suggestions.when_to_use: When delays needed in async code

lore.story: The False Promise - when the async declaration lies dormant
lore.moral: Declare intent only when action follows

lesson.short: Route import errors through Quantum Error Bridge to auto-create a PU and suggest fixes
lesson.long: Import errors often mean missing module paths or stale installs. Invoke Quantum Error Bridge: it converts the error into a ProblemSignature, attempts resolution, and if unresolved, generates a Processing Unit with proof gates for manual follow-up.

suggestions.description: Call QuantumErrorBridge.handle_error(error, context) with file/task info; let it auto-fix or create a PU
suggestions.when_to_use: On any ImportError/ModuleNotFound in repo code

suggestions.description: Ensure src/ is on sys.path; activate venv before running
suggestions.when_to_use: If bridge reports missing module path

lore.story: The Missing Thread – weave imports back into the tapestry via the quantum loom
lore.moral: When paths diverge, entangle them and observe
lore.culture_ship_wisdom: Every broken import is a request for coordination

lesson.short: Object/module doesn't have expected attribute - likely typo, wrong version, or stale import cache
lesson.long: AttributeErrors indicate: 1) typo in attribute name, 2) object is different type than expected, 3) stale .pyc files, 4) module version mismatch. Check spelling, type hints, and import statements first.

suggestions.description: Verify attribute name spelling and case
suggestions.example:
# Use dir(obj) to see available attributes
print(dir(my_object))

suggestions.description: Remove __pycache__ and .pyc files
Verify module is correct version
suggestions.example:
pip show module-name
pip install --upgrade module-name

suggestions.description: Use Quantum Error Bridge for systematic resolution

lore.story: The Missing Limb - when the body lacks what the mind expects
lore.moral: Observe what is, not what should be

errors: RuntimeError: asyncio.run() cannot be called from a running event loop
This event loop is already running

lesson.short: Async event loop conflicts - don't nest asyncio.run() calls or run in Jupyter/IPython
lesson.long: asyncio.run() creates a new event loop. Can't be called from within an existing loop (Jupyter, async functions, or nested calls). Use await directly in async context, or use asyncio.create_task() for concurrent tasks.

suggestions.description: If already in async context, use await instead of asyncio.run()
suggestions.example:
# Wrong:
async def foo():
    result = asyncio.run(bar())

# Right:
async def foo():
    result = await bar()

suggestions.description: Allow nested event loops (Jupyter/IPython)
suggestions.when_to_use: In Jupyter notebooks or interactive environments

suggestions.description: Get existing loop or create new one

lore.story: The Nested Loops - when time folds upon itself
lore.moral: One loop to rule them all, one loop to bind them

errors: NameError: global name '.*' is not defined

lesson.short: Variable/function used before definition or import
lesson.long: NameError means Python doesn't know about the name in current scope. Common causes: 1) typo, 2) missing import, 3) wrong scope, 4) circular dependency, 5) used before definition. Check imports and function/variable order.

suggestions.description: Verify all necessary imports are present
suggestions.example:
# Add missing imports at top of file
from typing import Any
import sys

suggestions.description: Verify variable/function name spelling
suggestions.example:
# Check for typos in variable names
# Python is case-sensitive
suggestions.when_to_use: When you think it should be defined

suggestions.description: Ensure variable is in correct scope
suggestions.example:
# Variables defined in functions aren't global
# Use 'global' or 'nonlocal' keywords if needed

suggestions.description: Use Quantum Error Bridge for complex cases
suggestions.example: python scripts/start_nusyq.py debug "NameError: name 'Any' is not defined"
suggestions.when_to_use: When error persists after simple fixes

lore.story: The Unnamed - when the word has no referent
lore.moral: Names must be bound before they can be invoked
